                         Population Survey.

                                    THE STATUS OF RESEARCH ON GROSS CHANGES
                                             IN THE LABOR FORCE
                                                                                     by Harvey J. Hilaski*


           One of the least known and least under-            Gordon Committee), appointed in late 1961 to
        stood series of data on the labor force are           conduct an exhaustive study of the official data
        "gross change" statistics, a b y - p r o d u c t of   system on employment and unemployment,
        the Current Population Survey (CPS), the source       strongly recommended that publication of the
        of the official monthly estimates of employment       series be resumed. 2/
        and unemployment.
                                                                 Because of the great potential value of the
          Gross change data show the labor force status       data and the active interest in them, the Bureau
       of persons not only for the current month, as          of the Census and the Bureau of Labor Statistics
       does the regular CPS, but also for the previous        have continued to examine the problem areas in
       month. The data thus permit the identification         order to improve the data and make them
       and measurement of the flow of persons who             generally available again for research and
       enter or withdraw from the labor force from one        analysis of labor force behavior.
       month to the next, those who remain in any given

       industry and occupation to another. Gross              progress. More specifically, it provides a
       changes, therefore, represent primarily a short        resume of the historical expansion of the body
       run "flow" rather than a "stock" of the labor          of gross change statistics over 20 years, a com-
       supply.                                                prehensive discussion of the potential uses of

          Because of serious statistical deficiencies,        efforts undertaken to resolve them, and an out-

       analytical purposes have been very limited._l/

       only from 1949-52.                                        The Bureau of the Census published gross

         Gross change data could aid in interpreting           A more intensive analysis of labor turnover
      changes in employment and unemployment                may be possible through the use of gross
      developments, as they indicate the nature of the      change data, since they show the source and
      shift between the employed and unemployed             magnitude of industry and occupational em-
      status of persons in any 2 consecutive months.        ployment flows as well as shifts between em-
      For example, if unemployment increases in a           ployment and unemployment. Moreover, the data
      given month, gross flow data could reveal             might be used to interpret important structural
      whether the change was due to the inability of        shifts in industries and occupations in terms of
      entrants to find work or to persons losing or         employment levels and flows.
      leaving their jobs. _4/ Similarly, if employment
      increases, the data can show whether the ad-            Because gross flow data show industry and
      ditional employed came from the unemployed            occupational employment shifts and thus the
      group or from those not previously in the labor       source of seasonal employment changes, they
      force.                                                could be important in assessing the nature of

      in the Employment and Earnings and Monthly            view of all unemployed persons for a given
      Report on the Labor Force. Data on the total          month; (3) they reflect only the over-the-month
      unemployed each month are broken down into            change in status, whereas the published series
      four components: those who lost their jobs,           refers to the the last status prior to unemploy-
      those who have voluntarily left their jobs, r e -     ment regardless of time; (4) they do not show
      entrants into the labor force, and new entrants       separately those who lost or those who left their
      (those without previous work experience) into         jobs, but rather the total number who have

      unemployed differ in that (1) they refer to the       status; and (5) entrants into unemployment from

      and not the total unemployed; (2) they show the       entrants and new entrants.

                                                      Technical Note

                                          Additional information concerning the preparation
                                          of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                          and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                          survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                          technical notes for each of these series, available
                                          from the Bureau of Labor Statistics free of charge.



       INTRODUCTION                                                       Relation between the household and payroll series


           The statistics in this periodical are compiled from                The household and payroll data supplement one
       three major sources: (1) household interviews, (2) pay-            another, each providing significant types of information
       roll reports from employers, and (3) administrative                that the other cannot suitably supply. Population charac-
       statistics of unemployment insurance systems.                      teristics, for example, are readily obtained only from
                                                                          the household survey whereas detailed industrial classi-
           Data based on household interviews are obtained from           fications can be reliably derived only from establish-
       a sample survey of the population 16 years of age and              ment reports.
       over. The survey is conducted each month by the Bureau
       of the Census for the Bureau of Labor Statistics and pro-              Data from these two sources differ from each other
       vides comprehensive data on the labor force, the em-               because of differences in definition and coverage, sources
       ployed and the unemployed, including such characteristics          of information, methods of collection, and estimating
       as age, sex, color, marital status, occupations, hours of          procedures. Sampling variability and response errors
       work, and duration of unemployment. The survey also                are additional reasons for discrepancies. The major
       provides data on the characteristics and past work ex-             factors which have a differential effect on levels and
       perience of those not in the labor force. The information          trends of the two series are as follows:
       is collected by trained interviewers from a sample of
       about 50,000 households, representing 449 areas in 863
                                                                             Employment
       counties and independent cities, with coverage in 50 States
       and the District of Columbia. The data collected are based            Coverage. The household survey definition of employ-
       on the activity or status reported for the calendar week           ment comprises wage and salary workers (including
       including the 12th of the month.                                   domestics and other private household workers), self-

           Payroll reports provide current information on wage
       and salary employment, hours, earnings, and labor turn-
                                                                       Shuttle Schedules
       over in nonagricultural establishments, by industry and
       geographic location.                                                 Two types of data collection schedules are used:
                                                                        Form BLS 790—Monthly Report on Employment, Pay-
       Federal-State Cooperation                                        roll, and Hours; and Form DL 1219—Monthly Report
                                                                       on Labor Turnover. These schedules are of the "shuttle"
          Under cooperative arrangements with State agencies,
                                                                       type, with space for each month of the calendar year.
       the respondent fills out a single employment or labor
                                                                       The collecting agency returns the schedule to the
       turnover reporting form, which is then used for national,
                                                                       respondent each month so that the next month's data can
       State, and area estimates. This eliminates duplicate re-
       porting on the part of respondents and, together with the       be entered. This procedure assures maximum com-
       use of identical techniques at the national and State           parability and accuracy of reporting, since the re-
       levels, insures maximum comparability of estimates.             spondent can see the figures he has reported for previous
                                                                       months.

      ployment insurance program. It includes some persons              ing a period of unemployment. A claimant who continues

      ployed in the payroll and household surveys. Excluded             insured unemployment figure.
      are persons who have exhausted their benefit rights, new
      workers who have not earned rights to unemployment                   Because of differences in State laws and procedures
      insurance, and persons losing jobs not covered by unem-           under which unemployment insurance programs are op-
      ployment insurance systems (agriculture, State and local          erated, State unemployment rates generally indicate, but
      government, domestic service, self-employment, unpaid             do not precisely measure, differences among the indi-

                                                            ATTENTION
            As discussed in the Technical Note, the Bureau periodically adjusts the industry employ-
      ment series to a recent benchmark to improve their accuracy. These adjustments may also
      affect the hours and earnings series because employment levels are used as weights. Industry
      data for all national series shown in this report have been adjusted to March 1967 benchmarks.
      Data from April 1967 forward are subject to revision at the time of the next benchmark.

           Beginning with the June 1968 and subsequent issues of Employment and Earnings and
      Monthly Report on the Labor Force, the national data in sections B, C, and D supersede
      those published in previous issues, as well as those appearing in the Handbook of Labor Statis-
      tics, 1968. Comparable data are published in Employment and Earnings Statistics for the
      United States, 1909-68, BLS Bulletin 131?.-6.
